variables.content:
Let v_{\mathrm{F}} be the monolayer graphene Dirac Fermi velocity and v_{\mathrm{F}}^{*}(\theta) be the moiré-band renormalized Fermi velocity computed in the Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) continuum model as a function of twist angle \theta; the BM continuum model yields quantitatively reliable estimates of v_{\mathrm{F}}^{*}(\theta) for experimental tBLG devices in the twist-angle range \theta\gtrsim1.15^\circ, in particular giving v_{\mathrm{F}}^{*}(\theta\approx1.5^\circ)\sim v_{\mathrm{F}}/4 and the rapid suppression of v_{\mathrm{F}}^{*} as \theta approaches the magic angle, so these BM predictions can be used in quantitative resistivity modeling for those angles.
      R. Bistritzer and A. H. MacDonald, Moiré bands in twisted double-layer graphene, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 108, 12233 (2011).

The two-valley continuum model of twisted bilayer graphene (TBG), which treats valley indices $\tau=\pm$ as effectively decoupled and describes low-energy moiré physics via the Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum Hamiltonian parametrized by the twist angle $\theta$, accurately captures the single-particle flat-band physics when the twist angle $\theta$ is sufficiently small so that intervalley scattering is negligible and is sufficiently close to the first magic angle $\theta^{*}\approx1.08^\circ$ so that (i) the eight active flat bands are narrowly separated from remote bands by appreciable gaps and (ii) the active-band bandwidths are comparable to or smaller than proximate energy scales (Rashba, Ising, sublattice $u$); outside this regime processes such as valley-mixing, remote-band hybridization, or large kinetic bandwidth invalidate the isolated-band projection and the subsequent conclusions.

The Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum moiré-band model, a continuum effective model parameterized by intralayer graphene parameters and interlayer tunneling amplitudes, provides the noninteracting low-energy band structure of twisted bilayer graphene (tBLG), and the Dirac-point band Fermi velocity v_F^*(θ) obtained from that noninteracting continuum model is to be treated as the bare (noninteracting) input parameter for subsequent low-energy many-body and phonon-transport calculations.
      R. Bistritzer and A.H. MacDonald, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 108 (2011) 12233.

For continuum-model electronic-structure calculations of magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene that explicitly incorporate lattice relaxation via atomic-registry-dependent interlayer coupling leading to enlarged AB/BA regions and shrunken AA regions, the computed low-energy flat-band manifold exhibits qualitatively increased bandwidth (broader flat bands) and increased energetic separation from remote bands in the reconstructed geometry relative to the unreconstructed rigid geometry, and this qualitative trend is robust to moderate variations of model parameters even when spatially non-uniform heterostrain is not included in the calculation; here "continuum model" refers to the Bistritzer–MacDonald-type moiré Hamiltonian augmented by a registry-dependent interlayer tunneling parameterization and a relaxation-derived displacement field.
      [18-21]

The noninteracting Bistritzer-MacDonald continuum model of $1.05^{\circ}$ twisted bilayer graphene, with Dirac velocity $v_{F}=10^{6}~\mathrm{m/s}$ in both graphene layers and intra- and inter-sublattice tunneling parameters $w_{0}=77~\mathrm{meV}$ and $w_{1}=110~\mathrm{meV}$, yields band dispersions and Bloch wave functions that reproduce the qualitative tunneling singularity phenomenology of interest, including inverse-root onsets, coherence peaks, and cone-shaped low-bias features, when used in momentum-conserving tunneling simulations. This approximation assumes that interaction-driven renormalizations in actual magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene do not qualitatively alter the geometric origin of the tangency-induced singularities.

The continuum Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) model of twisted bilayer graphene, after applying a layer-dependent momentum shift that restores moiré periodicity, can be represented in the magnetic-translation-irrep Landau-level basis at flux $\phi=2\pi$ and diagonalized after truncating to a finite number of Landau levels. For magic-angle geometric parameters and typical BM interlayer tunneling strengths, this numerical diagonalization (with sufficient Landau-level cutoff) reveals two low-energy flat bands per valley and spin that reappear (reenter) at $\phi=2\pi$ and are, for the parameter sets considered, separated from higher-energy passive bands by a sizable gap on the order of 40 meV. The dispersion and topology of these reentrant flat bands depend sensitively on the interlayer hopping parameters $(w_0,w_1)$: scanning $(w_0,w_1)$ produces a phase diagram with a crystalline regime (parameter region including the physical TBG values) where the two flat bands together have vanishing total Chern number, and a Landau-level-type regime (including the first chiral limit $w_0=0$) where each flat band individually carries Chern number $-1$.

For twisted bilayer graphene (tBLG) with twist angle θ measured in degrees, let θ_M denote the largest magic angle at which the noninteracting continuum moiré-band Dirac-point band velocity vanishes, and let v_F ≈ 10^8 cm/s be the monolayer-graphene Dirac velocity; then the noninteracting continuum Bistritzer–MacDonald moiré-band calculation of the low-energy Dirac-point Fermi velocity v_F^*(θ) is accurately approximated for 0<θ<3° by the linear formula
      $$
      v_F^*(\theta)\approx 0.5\,|\theta-\theta_M|\,v_F,
      $$
      with θ and θ_M in degrees, so that the numerical continuum-band outputs for v_F^*(θ) are captured to useful accuracy by this linear fit in the specified angular window.
      R. Bistritzer and A.H. MacDonald, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. USA 108 (2011) 12233; F. Wu, E. Hwang, S. Das Sarma, Phys. Rev. B 99 (2019) 165112.

For twisted bilayer graphene (tBLG) the noninteracting low-energy Dirac-point band Fermi velocity v_F^*(θ) produced by the Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum moiré-band model is strongly suppressed as the twist angle θ (measured in degrees) approaches the largest magic angle θ_M (the angle at which the noninteracting Dirac-point band velocity vanishes), and for 0<θ<3° this noninteracting moiré-band velocity is well described by the empirical linear approximation
      $$
      v_F^*(\theta)\approx 0.5\,|\theta-\theta_M|\,v_F,
      $$
      where v_F^*(θ) is the tBLG moiré-band Dirac-cone Fermi velocity, θ and θ_M are expressed in degrees, and v_F ≈ 10^8 cm/s is the monolayer-graphene Dirac velocity; this linear fit reproduces the numerical outputs of the continuum Bistritzer–MacDonald moiré-band calculation to useful accuracy in the stated angular window.

The chiral-model magic-angle twisted bilayer graphene Hamiltonian — defined as the continuum Bistritzer–MacDonald model with the same-sublattice interlayer tunneling terms (the AA and BB interlayer hoppings) set to zero and with the dimensionless interlayer-coupling parameter alpha tuned to a magic-angle value at which the zero-field bands are exactly flat — has two exactly dispersionless (zero-energy) bands that remain exactly flat for any applied uniform perpendicular magnetic field b when the units absorb the electron charge and set Planck's constant and the speed of light to unity (ℏ = c = 1).  Denoting by A_M > 0 the area of the moiré unit cell and by Φ = b A_M the magnetic flux per moiré unit cell, the A-sublattice-polarized flat band carries Chern number C = +1 and the B-sublattice-polarized flat band carries Chern number C = −1 for flux magnitude |Φ| less than the flux quantum Φ_0 = 2π (in these units).  The exact flatness and the sublattice-resolved Chern-number assignment hold for arbitrary commensurate flux values b = (p/q) (2π/A_M) with integers p,q.

Computed moiré band structures and density-of-states (DOS) profiles for twisted bilayer graphene as functions of twist angle $\theta$ (in particular for $\theta$ in the interval $1.3^\circ$–$1.9^\circ$) obtained from the Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum model with relaxation-corrected tunneling parameters ($t_{AA},t_{AB}$) and Fermi velocity $v_F$ (for example, the Koshino parametrization $t_{AA}=79.7\,$meV, $t_{AB}=97.5\,$meV, $v_F=7.98\times10^5\,$m/s) and a finite discretization mesh yield that the two moiré Dirac bands remain energetically isolated by superlattice gaps across that range for those chosen parameters and numerical resolution; however, the existence and numerical size of those gaps depends on model parameter choices, relaxation implementation, and numerical mesh resolution, so conclusions about gap closure when extrapolating toward the magic angle carry model-dependent uncertainty.

Modeling hBN-aligned twisted bilayer graphene near the magic angle $\theta_{\mathrm{M}}\simeq1.063^\circ$ by the Bistritzer–MacDonald moiré continuum model with interlayer tunneling parameters $u=0.077\ \mathrm{eV}$ and $u'=0.11\ \mathrm{eV}$ and adding a uniform sublattice-staggering on-site term of magnitude $\Delta/2$ to mimic hBN alignment reproduces the single-particle flat-band spectrum features relevant for computing the intrinsic capacitance; this continuum modeling neglects atomic relaxation, strain, lattice reconstruction, screening, and interaction-driven band renormalization, so quantitative predictions for the exact depth and location of capacitance extrema may differ in experimental devices.
      Bistritzer, R. & MacDonald, A. H., PNAS 108, 12233 (2011)

Consider the Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum model for twisted bilayer graphene represented in a plane-wave basis truncated to include plane waves up to the third honeycomb shell. Truncating the momentum-space basis in this way produces a finite Hamiltonian matrix of dimension 196\times196. The computational proposition used in the numerical study is: the eigenvalues and eigenvectors obtained by classical diagonalization of this 196\times196 truncated Hamiltonian provide an adequate representation of the low-energy moiré-band features of the continuum model (including twist-angle–dependent band narrowing and the emergence of near-flat bands at the magic-angle regime) for the twist angles studied, so that agreement between P-FQE numerical simulation and the truncated classical diagonalization reflects algorithmic correctness within the chosen finite basis.

Let the continuum Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) model for twisted bilayer graphene with interlayer hopping amplitude $w$, monolayer Fermi velocity $v$, moiré wave number $k_{\theta}=2k_D\sin(\theta/2)$, and BM dimensionless parameter $\alpha=w/(v k_{\theta})$ be given. For twist angles near the first magic angle and for electronic states with crystal momenta measured relative to each Dirac-cone label $I\in\{K_1,K_1',K_2,K_2'\}$, projecting the full BM Hamiltonian onto the twofold low-energy subspace associated with the lower BM band (using the normalized low-energy eigenstates $\Psi^{I}$ with normalization factor $1+6\alpha^{2}$) yields effective two-component Dirac-like band operators $c_{\boldsymbol{k}I s}$ with projected lower-band dispersion $\varepsilon_{\boldsymbol{k}}$ and two-component Dirac-band spinors $\phi_{\boldsymbol{k}}^{\eta}$ as the relevant electronic degrees of freedom. This projection neglects remote bands and assumes that coupling to higher-energy BM bands does not qualitatively change the low-energy layer and valley-resolved spinor structure used for electron–magnon matrix elements.

The Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) continuum moiré-band model is defined as the continuum two-layer Dirac Hamiltonian with interlayer tunneling parameters $w_{0},w_{1}$ and monolayer Dirac velocity $v_{F}$ and predicts a moiré-band Fermi velocity $v_{F}^{*}(\theta)$ at the moiré Dirac points as a function of twist angle $\theta$; with standard BM parameter choices the model yields a strong suppression of $v_{F}^{*}$ approaching zero at a 'magic' twist angle near $1^\circ$ and gives $v_{F}^{*}/v_{F}\approx 1/4$ at $\theta=1.5^\circ$ within the noninteracting continuum approximation, this prediction referring to the single-particle moiré band velocity in the absence of lattice relaxation or many-body self-energy corrections.
      R. Bistritzer and A. H. MacDonald, Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U.S.A. 108, 12233 (2011)

The Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) continuum moiré-band calculation, implemented as the two-layer continuum Dirac Hamiltonian with standard interlayer tunneling parameters and monolayer Dirac velocity $v_{F}$, predicts a strong suppression of the moiré-band Fermi velocity $v_{F}^{*}$ as the twist angle $\theta$ approaches the magic angle near $1^\circ$, with $v_{F}^{*}/v_{F}\to0$ at the magic angle and with a numerical value $v_{F}^{*}/v_{F}\approx 1/4$ at $\theta=1.5^\circ$ within the noninteracting BM model without lattice relaxation; since phonon-limited resistivity and its temperature coefficient scale as $1/v_{F}^{*2}$, this suppression implies up to $\sim 16$-fold enhancement of phonon-limited resistivity relative to untwisted monolayer graphene when $v_{F}^{*}$ is reduced into the small-$v_{F}^{*}$ regime.

Truncating the full Bistritzer–MacDonald continuum model for twisted bilayer graphene to the tripod (eight-state) Hamiltonian that retains one low-energy Dirac cone and its three nearest high-energy cones, and then diagonalizing the high-energy block and perturbatively projecting onto the low-energy doublet, yields an effective $2\times2$ Hamiltonian whose renormalized Dirac velocity $v^\ast$ depends on the combination $(A+v_F\Delta K)$. This truncation and perturbative projection (neglecting more distant shells and higher-order couplings) captures the dominant relaxation-induced renormalization of the low-energy Fermi velocity relevant for estimating the shift of the magic condition in the studied twist-angle window.

Within the single‑particle Bistritzer–MacDonald (BM) continuum model for magic‑angle twisted bilayer graphene, computed using a gauge‑invariant rational‑flux numerical formalism that diagonalizes the BM Hamiltonian in a magnetic Bloch basis at flux Φ = 2π (one flux quantum Φ0 per moiré unit cell), the low‑energy single‑particle spectrum at Φ = Φ0 reorganizes into eight well‑isolated, full‑density Bloch‑like bands (counting spin and valley flavors according to the model), each with bandwidths comparable to the zero‑field flat bands but with symmetry properties and single‑particle topological characteristics (as diagnosed by Wilson‑loop winding and gap Chern numbers) that differ from the zero‑flux bands; these Φ0 bands are single‑particle Bloch‑like bands (not conventional Landau levels) and the calculation is reported as exact within the BM‑model framework across 0 ≤ Φ ≤ Φ0 using the stated gauge‑invariant method.

A Bistritzer–MacDonald-type continuum single-particle model for twisted double bilayer graphene (tDBLG), implemented using a Bernal bilayer tight-binding parametrization and including an interlayer potential difference U to model the vertical electric field (the interlayer potential is applied as equal U/3 offsets between adjacent graphene layers), reproduces three key single-particle observables relevant to the experiments: (i) two narrow conduction and valence moiré mini-bands near the Fermi level whose band-structure van Hove singularities correspond to the experimentally observed conduction flat band (CFB) and valence flat band (VFB) peaks in the calculated density of states, (ii) the gate-voltage (vertical electric field) tunability by which both the CFB and VFB move relative to the chemical potential and by which their energetic separation increases with increasing applied vertical field, and (iii) the spatial delocalization of the CFB and the localization of higher-energy remote bands when the single-particle LDOS is projected onto the topmost graphene layer. However, the non-interacting continuum LDOS computed within this single-particle model does not develop the experimentally observed CFB splitting and Fermi-level LDOS suppression at filling ν ≈ 2, demonstrating that single-particle theory without electron-electron interactions cannot account for the correlated spectral reconstruction observed at ν ≈ 2.

The continuum moiré-band model for twisted bilayer graphene formulated by Bistritzer and MacDonald is parameterised by the graphene monolayer Fermi velocity $v_{F}$, interlayer tunnelling amplitudes $w_{AA}$ and $w_{AB}$ that control same-sublattice and different-sublattice interlayer hybridisation, and the twist angle $\theta$ that sets the moiré periodicity; implementing this continuum model with a chosen parameter set (including the experimentally determined $\theta$) is assumed to provide an accurate representation of the device's low-energy moiré miniband spectrum relevant to transverse magnetic focusing, i.e., that the model-predicted single-particle band energies $E(\mathbf{k})$, the locations of neutrality points and van Hove singularities in the density of states, and the Fermi-surface contours correspond to those in the physical sample within experimental uncertainty, while acknowledging that sample-specific effects (local strain, spatially varying interlayer registry, twist-angle inhomogeneity, many-body renormalisation, or edge reconstruction) are not included in the minimal continuum parameterisation and could produce deviations.

In modeling twisted bilayer graphene (TBG) near the nominal magic twist angle $\theta\approx1.05^\circ$, the continuum-model Hamiltonian parameterized by interlayer tunneling amplitudes $u_0$ and $u_1$ (here $u_0=79.7\,$meV and $u_1=97.5\,$meV) produces low-energy quasi-flat conduction bands whose computed dispersion yields an effective Fermi velocity $v_{\mathrm{eff}}$ (defined by the slope of the band dispersion near the Fermi level) that is strongly reduced compared to single-layer graphene (e.g., $v_{\mathrm{eff}}\sim0.04\,v_F$). The proposition is that these chosen continuum-model parameters and the resulting $v_{\mathrm{eff}}$ are accurate representations of realistic magic-angle samples for the fillings considered, i.e., that the continuum model with these numerical parameters captures the essential low-energy single-particle bandstructure relevant to plasmon calculations.
      Continuum-model references: Refs. [50,53–57] in the paper
